title: The First Man-Made Mountain
bullets: Djoser, first pharaoh of the Third Dynasty, moved the center of royal burial permanently from Abydos to the Saqqara necropolis west of the capital at Memphis.
His vizier Imhotep designed the Step Pyramid at Saqqara, a six-tiered structure rising 204 feet, the first large-scale stone building in the history of the world.
The Step Pyramid complex covered 37 acres with colonnades, ceremonial courts, and stone chapels, all translating temporary reed-and-mudbrick architecture into permanent limestone.
Beneath the pyramid, tunnels extending 3.5 miles held 40,000 stone vessels in alabaster, diorite, and basalt, many collected from earlier royal tombs at Abydos and Saqqara.
Underground chambers were lined with blue faience tiles imitating the woven reed-mat walls of the pharaoh's palace, making the temporary permanent for eternity.
Imhotep held the titles of vizier, high priest of Ra at Heliopolis, chief architect, and chief physician, the first non-royal individual in recorded history whose name was preserved.
Djoser's successor Sekhemkhet began a second step pyramid at Saqqara intended at seven tiers, but it was abandoned unfinished and its sealed alabaster sarcophagus was found empty in 1954.
The entire state apparatus, from nilometer measurements to nome-organized labor corvees to cedar shipments from Byblos, was now pointed at one goal: building the pharaoh's pyramid.
subtitle: Djoser's Step Pyramid — the first monumental stone building on earth

title: Sneferu's Laboratories
bullets: Sneferu founded the Fourth Dynasty around 2613 BCE and built more pyramid mass than any other pharaoh in Egyptian history, including his son Khufu.
His first pyramid at Meidum began as a step pyramid but partially collapsed when engineers tried to convert it to a true pyramid by filling in the steps with casing blocks.
The Bent Pyramid at Dahshur started at a steep 54-degree angle but engineers reduced it to 43 degrees midway up after cracks appeared, creating its distinctive kinked profile.
The Bent Pyramid retains most of its original smooth white limestone casing, the best-preserved exterior surface of any Egyptian pyramid.
The Red Pyramid at Dahshur used the proven 43-degree angle from the start and became the first successful true pyramid in the world, standing 344 feet tall around 2590 BCE.
Together Sneferu's three pyramids contain over 3.6 million cubic meters of stone, compared to the Great Pyramid's 2.6 million cubic meters.
The internal chambers of the Red Pyramid used a refined corbelled ceiling technique that would be taken much further in the Great Pyramid's design.
Later Egyptian tradition remembered Sneferu as a genial, accessible ruler called the beneficent king, in contrast to his son Khufu who was later portrayed as a tyrant.
subtitle: three experimental pyramids and the engineering breakthroughs they required

title: The Great Pyramid
bullets: Khufu's Great Pyramid at Giza was built from approximately 2.3 million limestone blocks averaging 2.5 tons each, with a total weight of about 6 million tons.
At 481 feet tall, the Great Pyramid was the tallest structure on Earth for 3,800 years, not surpassed until Lincoln Cathedral's spire was completed around 1311 CE.
The foundation was leveled to within 2.1 centimeters across the entire 756-foot base, probably using water-filled channels cut into the bedrock to establish a level surface.
Hemiunu, Khufu's vizier and grandson of Sneferu, designed the pyramid and held the title Overseer of All Construction Projects of the King.
The Grand Gallery inside the pyramid is a corbelled passage 153 feet long and 28 feet high, one of the most extraordinary architectural spaces in the ancient world.
The workers' village at Giza, excavated by Mark Lehner from 1988, contained bakeries, breweries, and a copper workshop, demolishing the slave-labor myth.
Workers' cemetery bones show healed fractures, successful limb amputations, and brain surgery attempts, proving the workforce received serious medical treatment.
A 143-foot ceremonial solar barque of Lebanese cedar was found disassembled in 1,224 pieces in a sealed pit beside the Great Pyramid in 1954.
In 2017, cosmic-ray muon tomography revealed a previously unknown large void above the Grand Gallery, at least 100 feet long, with an unknown purpose.
subtitle: Khufu's Great Pyramid — how 2.3 million blocks became perfection

title: Sphinx and Sunset at Giza
bullets: Khafre's pyramid at 471 feet appears taller than his father Khufu's because he chose a slightly higher spot on the Giza plateau, probably an intentional optical illusion.
The Great Sphinx of Giza was carved from a single natural limestone outcrop, 240 feet long and 66 feet high, with the body of a lion and the face of a pharaoh.
Around 1400 BCE, Prince Thutmose fell asleep between the Sphinx's buried paws and dreamed it promised him the throne if he cleared the sand; he became Thutmose IV.
Khafre's valley temple was built from massive polished red granite blocks weighing over 100 tons, with no carved decoration, conveying power through sheer stone mass.
A diorite statue of Khafre seated with Horus spreading his wings behind his head is one of the masterpieces of Egyptian art, now in the Egyptian Museum in Cairo.
Menkaure built the third and smallest Giza pyramid at 213 feet, less than half the height of Khufu's, with lower courses cased in expensive red granite.
Meresankh III, granddaughter of Khufu and wife of Khafre, was buried in one of the most elaborately decorated mastabas on the plateau with carved statues and painted scenes.
After Menkaure, his son Shepseskaf broke with pyramid tradition entirely, building his tomb at South Saqqara in the shape of a massive rectangular sarcophagus.
subtitle: Khafre, the Sphinx, and the end of the pyramid-building frenzy

title: The Sun Kings
bullets: The Fifth Dynasty shifted Egypt's theological focus from the pharaoh's personal divinity to the cult of Ra, the sun god, centered at the ancient religious capital of Heliopolis.
Every Fifth Dynasty pharaoh added Son of Ra to the royal titulary, a title that would be carried by every subsequent Egyptian pharaoh for the next two thousand years.
Six Fifth Dynasty pharaohs built sun temples at Abu Gorab with open courtyards centered on massive squat obelisks, open to the sky rather than enclosed like traditional temples.
Sahure launched the first documented Egyptian naval expedition to the Land of Punt on the Horn of Africa, returning with myrrh, frankincense, electrum, and exotic animals.
The Abusir Papyri are the oldest surviving administrative documents from ancient Egypt, showing in granular detail how pyramid mortuary cults were provisioned and maintained.
Ptahhotep composed the Instructions of Ptahhotep under Pharaoh Djedkare Isesi, a collection of thirty-seven maxims on ethics and leadership that predates the Book of Proverbs by a thousand years.

Djedkare Isesi reigned nearly forty years and began appointing provincial officials from outside the royal family, unknowingly creating the class of hereditary nomarchs that would hollow out pharaonic authority.
Unas, the last Fifth Dynasty pharaoh, inscribed the first Pyramid Texts on his burial chamber walls at Saqqara, the oldest known religious literature in the world.
The Palermo Stone, probably carved during this era, records year-by-year annals including Nile flood levels, temple foundations, and cattle counts from the First through Fifth Dynasties.
subtitle: smaller pyramids, sun temples, and the rise of the priesthood

title: The Long Reign
bullets: Teti founded the Sixth Dynasty around 2345 BCE and was reportedly murdered by his own bodyguard, the first known regicide in Egyptian history according to the historian Manetho.
Weni the Elder rose from minor functionary to army commander under Pepi I, leading five campaigns into southern Canaan with an army including Medjay mercenaries from Nubia.
Harkhuf led four trading expeditions deep into Nubia from Elephantine, reaching the distant land of Yam around the Third Cataract in what is now Sudan.
The young Pepi II wrote Harkhuf a letter demanding his dancing dwarf be brought safely to court, one of the most human documents from the ancient world.
Pepi II reigned for approximately ninety-four years, by far the longest confirmed reign in recorded history, ascending the throne at about age six.
Provincial nomarchs accumulated hereditary power throughout the Sixth Dynasty, building elaborate tombs in their home provinces and treating their nomes as personal territories.
The cumulative cost of maintaining daily offerings for every dead pharaoh's mortuary cult since Djoser had been growing for three hundred years, draining the treasury.

The Pyramid Texts grew to over 800 individual spells by the end of the Sixth Dynasty, describing two parallel afterlife journeys: joining Ra in the solar barque and ruling as Osiris in the underworld.
subtitle: Pepi II rules for decades as central authority slowly erodes

title: The World Falls Apart
bullets: Pepi II's death around 2184 BCE left a power vacuum that shattered Egypt into effectively independent nomes, ending five centuries of centralized Old Kingdom rule.
The 4.2-kiloyear drought event around 2200 BCE caused repeated low Nile floods and crop failures, devastating the basin irrigation system that depended on reliable annual flooding.
The Akkadian Empire in Mesopotamia collapsed at almost exactly the same time under the same environmental stress, making this one of history's first climate-driven political crises.
A powerful family at Herakleopolis established the Ninth and Tenth Dynasties in the north, while Intef I founded the Eleventh Dynasty at Thebes in Upper Egypt around 2130 BCE.
Ankhtifi, nomarch of Hierakonpolis and Edfu, claimed all of Upper Egypt was dying of hunger to such a degree that everyone had come to eating his children.
The Admonitions of Ipuwer describes social inversion in apocalyptic terms: the river is blood, the wealthy mourn, and gold and lapis lazuli are strung on the necks of slave girls.
The Dialogue of a Man with His Soul is the oldest known text dealing with existential despair and whether life is worth living in a collapsing society.
The Coffin Texts democratized the afterlife by adapting Pyramid Text spells for non-royal coffins, introducing moral judgment before Osiris and the monster Ammit who devoured unworthy hearts.
subtitle: famine, civil war, and the collapse of the Old Kingdom

title: Reunification
bullets: Mentuhotep II of Thebes conquered the Herakleopolitan dynasty around 2040 BCE, reunifying Egypt after over a century of civil war and launching the Middle Kingdom.
A mass grave at Deir el-Bahri containing about sixty soldiers with arrow wounds and crushed skulls provides evidence that the reunification war was real and violent.
Mentuhotep II built his mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahri in a terraced design cut into the cliff face, breaking with the Old Kingdom pyramid tradition entirely.
The temple's location at Thebes rather than Memphis declared that the center of Egyptian power had shifted south, a precedent that would hold through the New Kingdom.
Hatshepsut built her famous mortuary temple directly adjacent to Mentuhotep II's five hundred years later, echoing his terraced cliff-face design on a grander scale.
Mentuhotep II reorganized provincial administration, replacing hereditary nomarchs with loyal appointees, learning the lesson of the Old Kingdom's collapse.
The Old Kingdom's most durable creation was not the pyramids but the idea that Egypt was a unified, eternal state maintained by a divine pharaoh upholding ma'at.
The era produced the calendar (three seasons of four thirty-day months plus five epagomenal days) that through Julian and Gregorian reforms became the calendar the modern world uses.
subtitle: Mentuhotep II reunites Egypt and launches the Middle Kingdom
